meta.name: Mixed Opioid + Benzodiazepine Overdose — Partial Response to Naloxone, Continued BVM

patient.name: Tyler McClain
patient.chiefComplaint: Found unresponsive in a public restroom by a maintenance worker; lips cyanotic, slow shallow breathing, pinpoint pupils, and visible signs of recent IV drug use.

history.hpi: 32-year-old male was found unresponsive in the locked stall of a coffee shop restroom approximately 10 minutes ago. The maintenance worker forced the lock, found the patient slumped on the toilet with a tourniquet still on his arm, an empty syringe on the floor, and a pill bottle of alprazolam (prescribed to a different person) open on the floor. Patient is unresponsive to voice and shake; minimal response to noxious stimulus. Per the maintenance worker, no naloxone was given prior to EMS. Patient is otherwise unidentified — no ID on his person but a phone is on the floor. No history available beyond the scene.
pastMedical: Scene findings consistent with chronic IV drug use (multiple track marks, recent injection)
medications: Unknown — bottle of alprazolam (prescribed to someone else) found at the scene
history.events: Found unresponsive in coffee-shop restroom stall; tourniquet on arm; empty syringe and alprazolam pill bottle (prescribed to another person) on the floor; maintenance worker called 911.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 32-year-old male, unresponsive, found in a public restroom. Possible overdose. PD en route. Stage 1 response.
scene.location: Coffee shop public restroom, single-stall configuration. Patient on the floor between toilet and wall, slumped over. Tight quarters; will need to extricate to corridor before working.
scene.safety: Scene safe with PD secondary to staff. Standard universal precautions; high needlestick risk — visible needle on floor. Recommend gloves, eye protection, and careful handling of all surfaces.
scene.bystanders: Maintenance worker who found patient and the coffee shop manager. No witnesses to use; worker did not give naloxone.
visualCues: Young male slumped on bathroom floor, head against wall
Tourniquet still in place on left arm above the elbow with visible recent injection site and oozing puncture
Empty 1 mL syringe with brown residue on the floor
Open prescription bottle of alprazolam (someone else's name) with several pills still inside
Phone on the floor; no wallet/ID visible

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives to a young man unresponsive on the floor of a coffee-shop restroom with the classic opioid toxidrome — pinpoint pupils, hypoventilation, cyanosis — AND an open bottle of alprazolam belonging to someone else, suggesting a polysubstance ingestion. The teaching point here is foundational: airway, breathing, oxygenation FIRST; naloxone is an adjunct. With a benzodiazepine on board the patient may not fully wake even with adequate naloxone, and continued BVM ventilation is the right answer — RSI is NOT indicated as long as oxygenation and ventilation are adequate with BVM and a basic adjunct.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Partially obstructed — snoring respirations from posterior tongue position; jaw flaccid
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 4, shallow, irregular; minimal chest rise
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong, regular radial pulse; cool, dry skin; cap refill 2 sec

skin.color: central cyanosis at lips and nailbeds

clinicalPresentation.pupils: Pinpoint (~1 mm), minimally reactive — opioid toxidrome
otherFindings: Multiple old and one fresh track mark left antecubital
Empty syringe with brown residue (heroin/fentanyl suspect)
Alprazolam (someone else's name) bottle on floor — polysubstance
No tongue bite, no incontinence, no obvious trauma

expectedActions.shortAction: Scene safety; PPE; remove tourniquet; secure needle (sharps container)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose step 5 + Toxicology Universal — needlestick risk; safe handling.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Be aware that unsecured hypodermic needles may be on scene if the intravenous route may have been used by the patient, and that there is a higher risk of needle sticks during the management of this patient population which may also have an increased incidence of blood-borne pathogens.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; recognize opioid toxidrome (resp depression, miosis, AMS)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Inclusion + Toxidromes — opioid toxidrome with respiratory depression is the diagnostic trigger.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Patients exhibiting decreased mental status, and respiratory depression of all age groups with known or suspected opioid use or abuse. Lack of miosis (pinpoint pupils) is not a reliable sign for ruling out opioid exposure, although its presence is consistent with such exposure. Pertinent Assessment Findings: Miosis (pinpoint pupils); Respiratory depression; Decreased mental status.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reposition (head tilt/chin lift); insert NPA (gag intact)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatments 1 — critical resuscitation (airway, oxygen, circulation) BEFORE naloxone.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Critical resuscitation (opening and/or maintaining the airway, provision of oxygen, ensuring adequate circulation) should be performed prior to naloxone administration. Therapeutic interventions to support the patient's airway, breathing, and circulation should be initiated prior to the administration of naloxone.

expectedActions.shortAction: BVM ventilation 10–12/min with PEEP 5; target SpO2 94–98%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 5 + Patient Safety #3 — airway management is primary; oxygenation/ventilation is the goal regardless of naloxone dose.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regardless of the doses of naloxone administered, airway management with provision of adequate oxygenation and ventilation is the primary goal in patients with confirmed or suspected opioid overdose. EMS clinicians should be prepared to initiate airway management before, during, and after naloxone administration and to provide appropriate airway support until the patient has adequate respiratory effort.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous SpO2 + waveform capnography
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Documentation + Universal Tox Treatment 7 — capnography for respiratory decompensation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Pulse oximetry (oxygen saturation) and, if available, capnometry or capnography.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia as concurrent cause of AMS
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Assessment 3 + AMS — assess for other etiologies including hypoglycemia.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Assess the patient for other etiologies of altered mental status including hypoxia (pulse oximetry less than 94%), hypoglycemia, hypotension, and traumatic head injury.

transitions.if: Naloxone given; partial response (RR up, mentation still ↓)
transitions.condition: After airway management initiated, naloxone is administered (titrated low dose). Respiratory rate improves but mentation remains depressed because of the benzodiazepine component — continued BVM/supportive care required.
Crew administers naloxone IN without first managing airway/ventilation; patient remains hypoxic during onset window; SpO2 falls further; risk of cardiac arrest.

phases.name: Naloxone given; partial response
phases.description: After establishing airway and starting BVM, naloxone 0.4 mg IV is given (titrated low to avoid abrupt withdrawal). Within 2 minutes the respiratory rate improves to 14 with adequate tidal volume on its own, SpO2 climbs to 96% on supplemental O2, but the patient does not fully wake — he is groggy, eye-opens to pain, slurred 1–2 word responses. This pattern is classic for a polysubstance ingestion (opioid + benzo): naloxone reverses the opioid, but the benzodiazepine remains active. Continued airway monitoring and supportive care are correct; RSI is NOT indicated because the patient is now ventilating adequately and oxygenating well, and flumazenil is contraindicated.
phases.triggerCondition: Naloxone reversed the opioid component (respiratory rate restored), but the patient remains depressed from benzodiazepine on board. BVM no longer needed; continue supportive care, monitoring, transport.

clinicalPresentation.pupils: 3 mm and reactive — slightly dilated from pinpoint
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Withdraws to pain, sluggish to verbal
otherFindings: Naloxone 0.4 mg IV given titrated low
RR 14 spontaneous, SpO2 96% on supplemental O2
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (eye opens to noxious stim; slurred 1-word responses)

expectedActions.shortAction: Naloxone 0.4 mg IV (titrate to adequate respiratory effort, NOT to wake-up)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 3a + Patient Safety #2 — incrementally titrate naloxone to reverse respiratory depression; lowest possible dose to avoid precipitating withdrawal.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If the patient has respiratory depression from a confirmed or suspected opioid overdose, consider naloxone administration. The administration of the initial dose or subsequent doses can be incrementally titrated until respiratory depression is reversed. Adults: The typical initial adult dose ranges between Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV, IM, up to a dose of 4 mg IN or 5 mg ETT. Patient Safety: Patients with altered mental status secondary to an opioid overdose may become agitated or violent following naloxone administration due to opioid withdrawal therefore the goal is to use the lowest dose as possible to avoid precipitating withdrawal.

expectedActions.shortAction: Maintain NPA + supplemental O2; do NOT push to RSI — patient is ventilating adequately
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 5 — airway management with adequate oxygenation/ventilation is the primary goal; advanced airway only if BVM/supportive care fails.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regardless of the doses of naloxone administered, airway management with provision of adequate oxygenation and ventilation is the primary goal in patients with confirmed or suspected opioid overdose. EMS clinicians should be prepared to initiate airway management before, during, and after naloxone administration and to provide appropriate airway support until the patient has adequate respiratory effort.

expectedActions.shortAction: Do NOT give flumazenil — can precipitate refractory seizures in benzo-dependent patient
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tox Universal Notes #4 — flumazenil is not indicated in suspected benzodiazepine overdose due to seizure risk.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Notes/Educational Pearls: Flumazenil is not indicated in a suspected benzodiazepine overdose as it can precipitate refractory/intractable seizures if the patient is a benzodiazepine dependent patient.

expectedActions.shortAction: Monitor for recurrent respiratory depression — naloxone wears off in ~30–90 min
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Patient Safety #1 — naloxone effect may end within an hour; opioids often last much longer; monitor closely.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Clinical duration of naloxone: The clinical opioid reversal effect of naloxone is limited and may end within an hour whereas opioids often have a duration of 4 hours or longer. Monitor the patient for recurrent respiratory depression and decreased mental status.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport with continuous monitoring; bring pill bottle and syringe (in sharps) to ED
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tox Universal Treatment 9 — bring all medications/agents found; aids ED in identification.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: When indicated, identify specific medication taken (including immediate release vs sustained release), time of ingestion, dose, and quantity. When appropriate, bring all medications (prescribed and not prescribed) found in the environment.

expectedActions.shortAction: Coordinate with PD; document scene findings and chain of custody for evidence
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tox Universal step 14 — law enforcement awareness of weapons/drugs; document.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Law enforcement should have checked for weapons and drugs, but you may need to recheck. Obtain any other pertinent patient history.

transitions.condition: RR adequate, SpO2 96%, BP stable. Patient remains depressed but is supporting his own airway with NPA. Transport with continuous monitoring.
Crew gives 2 mg naloxone bolus instead of titrating; patient awakens combatively into precipitous opioid withdrawal — agitation, vomiting, possible aspiration, and patient pulls IV.

phases.name: Transport with monitoring
phases.description: Patient transported with continuous SpO2, EtCO2, cardiac monitor, NPA in place, supplemental O2. He remains depressed (benzo on board) but is ventilating adequately and oxygenating well. Crew watches for recurrent opioid effect (naloxone wearing off) and is prepared to repeat dose if respiratory depression recurs.
phases.triggerCondition: Patient stable on supportive care; transport to ED for further evaluation, observation period, and benzodiazepine clearance.

otherFindings: Stable; no recurrent respiratory depression yet
Pill bottle and syringe (in sharps) en route with patient

expectedActions.shortAction: Repeat naloxone 0.4 mg IV/IM if RR drops < 10 or SpO2 falls
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 3a + 4 — high-potency opioids may require repeat dosing; titrate to respiratory effort.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The administration of the initial dose or subsequent doses can be incrementally titrated until respiratory depression is reversed. High-potency opioids may require higher and/or more frequently administered doses of naloxone to reverse respiratory depression and/or to maintain adequate respirations.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document doses, route, response, total naloxone given, ongoing assessment
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Documentation — naloxone dose/route/response, number of doses required, ongoing airway assessment.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Rapid and accurate identification of signs and symptoms of opioid poisoning; Airway management; Pulse oximetry (oxygen saturation) and, if available, capnometry or capnography; Blood glucose assessment; Naloxone dose and route of administration; Clinical response to medication administration; Number of doses of naloxone to achieve a clinical response.

phases.name: Naloxone IN without airway support
phases.description: Crew jumped to naloxone IN without first opening the airway or giving BVM ventilation. Patient remained apneic during the 3–5 minute onset window for IN naloxone, SpO2 fell into the 60s, EtCO2 rose into the 70s, and the patient developed bradycardia from hypoxia. Crew now must rescue: airway adjunct + BVM with PEEP, give IV naloxone, and watch for arrest.
phases.triggerCondition: Naloxone given without simultaneous airway management; patient remained hypoxic during onset window and progressed to severe hypoxemia and bradycardia.

expectedActions.shortAction: NPA + two-person BVM with PEEP NOW; ventilate to SpO2 > 94%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 5 + Patient Safety #3 — airway management is primary; naloxone is adjunct.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regardless of the doses of naloxone administered, airway management with provision of adequate oxygenation and ventilation is the primary goal in patients with confirmed or suspected opioid overdose. EMS clinicians should be prepared to initiate airway management before, during, and after naloxone administration and to provide appropriate airway support until the patient has adequate respiratory effort.

expectedActions.shortAction: Naloxone 0.4–1 mg IV titrated to adequate respiratory effort (IV more predictable than IN)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 7 — IV provides more predictable bioavailability and titration flexibility.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Naloxone administration via the intravenous route provides more predictable bioavailability and flexibility in dosing and titration. The administration of the initial dose or subsequent doses can be incrementally titrated until respiratory depression is reversed.

expectedActions.shortAction: If pulse lost: CPR per Cardiac Arrest protocol; naloxone has NO benefit in arrest
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Treatment 3g — naloxone has no benefit in cardiac arrest; do not delay CPR/ventilation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Naloxone has no benefit in the treatment of cardiac arrest. Do not delay other interventions such as chest compressions and ventilations.

phases.name: Excessive naloxone → precipitated withdrawal
phases.description: Crew gave 2 mg of naloxone IV as a single bolus instead of titrating low. Within 60 seconds the patient awakens combatively, vomits, and tries to rip out the IV. This is iatrogenic precipitated opioid withdrawal — protocol explicitly warns against it. Crew must protect airway from aspiration, restrain only as needed for safety, and continue supportive care until benzo wears off as well.
phases.triggerCondition: Excessive naloxone bolus given (full dose rather than titrated low); patient woke combatively into precipitated opioid withdrawal with vomiting and agitation.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Tachycardic; warm and diaphoretic with withdrawal sympathomimetic surge
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Combative, swinging arms, trying to sit up
otherFindings: Acute precipitated withdrawal: agitation, vomiting, diaphoresis, mydriasis
Vomitus on the floor — aspiration risk
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Get … off … me! What did you do! I need to leave!

expectedActions.shortAction: Position to side; suction airway; protect from aspiration
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tox Universal — aspiration risk with vomiting in AMS/withdrawal patient.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Monitor patient airway, breathing, pulse oximetry, EtCO2 for adequate ventilation as they may change over time. Repeat vital signs often. Monitor level of consciousness.

expectedActions.shortAction: Verbal reassurance; explain naloxone reversed an overdose; coach to stay still
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Patient Safety #2 — be prepared for combative behavior, take measures to maintain scene safety.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Patients with altered mental status secondary to an opioid overdose may become agitated or violent following naloxone administration due to opioid withdrawal therefore the goal is to use the lowest dose as possible to avoid precipitating withdrawal. Be prepared for this potential scenario and take the appropriate measures in advance to ensure and maintain scene safety.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport — naloxone wears off and opioid + benzo can re-emerge; AMA risky
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Opioid Overdose Patient Safety #1 — naloxone may wear off within an hour; opioids last 4+ hours; risk of re-narcotization.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Clinical duration of naloxone: The clinical opioid reversal effect of naloxone is limited and may end within an hour whereas opioids often have a duration of 4 hours or longer. Monitor the patient for recurrent respiratory depression and decreased mental status.
